questions.question: Find the maximum subarray sum
questions.description: Given an integer array, find the contiguous subarray with the largest sum using Kadane's algorithm.

questions.question: Two Sum problem
questions.description: Given an array of integers and a target, return indices of two numbers that add up to the target.

questions.question: Merge sorted arrays
questions.description: Merge two sorted arrays into one sorted array in linear time.

questions.question: Product of array except self
questions.description: Compute an array where each element is the product of all other elements without using division.

questions.question: Find the missing number
questions.description: Find the missing number in an array of size n containing numbers from 1 to n.

questions.question: Rotate array by k positions
questions.description: Rotate an array to the right by k steps in O(1) extra space.

questions.question: Find duplicates in array
questions.description: Find all duplicates in an array where elements are within a given range.

questions.question: Container with most water
questions.description: Find two lines that together form a container holding the maximum amount of water.

questions.question: Trapping rain water
questions.description: Compute how much water can be trapped after raining using elevation heights.

questions.question: 3Sum problem
questions.description: Find all unique triplets in an array that sum to zero.

questions.question: Move zeroes to end
questions.description: Move all zeroes in an array to the end while maintaining relative order of non-zero elements.

questions.question: Find first and last position of target
questions.description: Find the starting and ending position of a target value in a sorted array using binary search.

questions.question: Tell me about yourself
questions.description: Structure your answer using the present-past-future formula highlighting relevant experience.

questions.question: Why do you want to work here?
questions.description: Research the company's mission, culture, and products to show genuine interest and alignment.

questions.question: Describe a challenging project
questions.description: Use the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action, Result) to describe a technically challenging project.

questions.question: How do you handle conflicts?
questions.description: Show emotional intelligence and conflict resolution skills with a specific example using STAR.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you failed
questions.description: Describe a genuine failure, what you learned, and how you improved, showing ownership and growth.

questions.question: Describe your ideal work environment
questions.description: Explain the culture, team structure, and work style that brings out your best performance.

questions.question: What are your greatest strengths?
questions.description: Highlight 2-3 technical or soft skills with specific examples that demonstrate each strength.

questions.question: What are your weaknesses?
questions.description: Be honest about a genuine weakness and describe the concrete steps you take to improve it.

questions.question: Tell me about a time you showed leadership
questions.description: Describe a situation where you led a team, initiative, or technical decision with measurable impact.

questions.question: How do you handle criticism?
questions.description: Show that you receive feedback constructively by describing a specific instance of acting on feedback.

questions.question: Describe a time you went above and beyond
questions.description: Share an example of exceeding expectations, taking initiative, or delivering extra value.

questions.question: What motivates you at work?
questions.description: Explain what drives you professionally, such as learning, impact, collaboration, or solving hard problems.

questions.question: How do you stay updated with technology?
questions.description: Describe your learning habits including blogs, courses, side projects, and community involvement.

questions.question: Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

ai.name: Artificial Intelligence
ai.description: The simulation of human intelligence by machines, encompassing learning, reasoning, problem-solving, perception, and language understanding. The broadest field in computer science aiming to create intelligent systems.
ai.overview: Artificial Intelligence is the broad discipline of creating intelligent machines that can perceive, reason, learn, and act. Founded at the Dartmouth Conference in 1956, AI has undergone several waves: symbolic AI (expert systems, logic), statistical learning (ML), and the current deep learning revolution powered by large neural networks and massive data. AI encompasses subfields including machine learning, natural language processing, computer vision, robotics, knowledge representation, automated reasoning, and planning. Modern AI achievements include GPT-4, DALL-E, AlphaFold, self-driving cars, and game-playing systems (AlphaGo, AlphaZero). AI raises profound technical challenges (generalization, interpretability, alignment) and societal questions (ethics, fairness, job displacement). The field is advancing rapidly with transformer architectures, foundation models, and multimodal AI systems.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between AI, ML, and Deep Learning?
interviewQuestions.answer: AI is the broad field of creating intelligent machines. ML is a subset of AI where systems learn from data without explicit programming. Deep Learning is a subset of ML using multi-layered neural networks to learn hierarchical representations. All DL is ML, all ML is AI, but not vice versa. Example: rule-based chess AI is not ML; linear regression is ML; CNNs for image classification is DL.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the A* search algorithm and its optimality.
interviewQuestions.answer: A* combines the cost to reach a node (g) and a heuristic estimate to the goal (h). It maintains an open set prioritized by f = g + h. A* is optimal if the heuristic is admissible (never overestimates) and consistent (h(n) <= c(n,a,n') + h(n')). A* is complete if branching factor is finite. Dijkstra is A* with h=0.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between supervised, unsupervised, and reinforcement learning?
interviewQuestions.answer: Supervised: learns from labeled data (input, output pairs), goal is to map inputs to outputs. Unsupervised: discovers hidden patterns in unlabeled data (clustering, dimensionality reduction). Reinforcement: learns through interaction by maximizing cumulative reward, no labels, learns from consequences of actions.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the attention mechanism and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.answer: Attention allows models to focus on relevant parts of the input when producing output. It computes weighted sums of values based on query-key similarity. Self-attention (transformers) allows each position to attend to all positions, capturing long-range dependencies without recurrence. Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel. Attention enabled LLMs by solving the long-range dependency bottleneck of RNNs.

interviewQuestions.question: What is overfitting and how do you prevent it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Overfitting is when a model learns training data too well, including noise, and fails to generalize to new data. Signs: high training accuracy but poor validation accuracy. Prevention: more training data, cross-validation, regularization (L1, L2), dropout (neural networks), early stopping, reducing model complexity, feature selection, data augmentation, ensemble methods.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff.
interviewQuestions.answer: Bias is error from overly simplistic models (underfitting) — wrong assumptions about the data. Variance is error from overly complex models (overfitting) — sensitive to training data fluctuations. High bias: models miss relevant patterns. High variance: models learn noise. The tradeoff: increasing complexity reduces bias but increases variance. Optimal model balances both for minimum total error.

interviewQuestions.question: What are generative vs discriminative models?
interviewQuestions.answer: Generative models learn P(X, Y) — joint distribution of inputs and labels — and can generate new data. Examples: Naive Bayes, GANs, VAEs, diffusion models. Discriminative models learn P(Y|X) — conditional probability of label given input — focusing on decision boundary. Examples: logistic regression, SVM, neural networks. Generative models can create data; discriminative models typically have higher classification accuracy.

bestPractices: Start simple — use a simple baseline (linear model, heuristic) before complex deep learning
Always split data into train/validation/test sets — never let test data influence model decisions
Use cross-validation when working with small datasets
Normalize/standardize features for gradient-based learning methods
Use appropriate evaluation metrics — accuracy is misleading for imbalanced datasets
Monitor for data leakage — ensure no information from the future/test set leaks into training
Version control datasets, models, and experiments (DVC, MLflow, Weights & Biases)
Use reproducible random seeds for experiments
Log all experiments: hyperparameters, metrics, model artifacts
Document assumptions, data sources, preprocessing steps, and model limitations
Start with interpretable models when possible — explainability is crucial for production
Test on edge cases and distribution shifts before deployment
Implement monitoring for model drift and data drift in production
Follow ethical AI guidelines: fairness audits, bias testing, privacy protection
Use appropriate hardware — start with CPU/small models, scale to GPU/TPU as needed
Keep up with research — the field advances rapidly, follow arXiv, Twitter, conferences
Prefer simple solutions: if a rule-based system works, don't build a neural network
Always baseline against random/naive predictions to validate your model adds value

Design RL agents for robotics, games, recommendation systems, and autonomous decision-making.
Research how to ensure AI systems are safe, aligned with human values, robust, and interpretable.

cpp.name: C++
cpp.description: A powerful systems programming language that extends C with object-oriented, generic, and functional features. Used in game engines, embedded systems, high-frequency trading, and performance-critical applications.
cpp.overview: C++ is a statically typed, compiled, multi-paradigm programming language created by Bjarne Stroustrup in 1985 as an enhancement to C. It provides low-level memory control, high-level abstractions, and zero-cost abstractions — you only pay for what you use. C++ powers AAA game engines (Unreal Engine), web browsers (Chrome, Firefox), operating systems, databases (MySQL, MongoDB), financial trading systems, and embedded devices. Modern C++ (C++11 through C++23) adds lambda expressions, smart pointers, move semantics, concurrency support, ranges, coroutines, and modules. With its unmatched combination of performance and expressiveness, C++ remains the language of choice when hardware control and efficiency matter most.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between pointer and reference in C++?
interviewQuestions.answer: Pointers can be reassigned and can be null; use * and -> operators; support pointer arithmetic. References cannot be reassigned (bound at initialization), must always refer to a valid object, and use . operator (transparent syntax). References are safer but less flexible than pointers. Use references for function parameters and return values where rebinding is not needed; use pointers when nullability or dynamic allocation is required.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain virtual functions and the vtable.
interviewQuestions.answer: Virtual functions enable runtime polymorphism via dynamic dispatch. The compiler creates a vtable (virtual table) for each class with virtual functions — an array of function pointers. Each object has a vptr (hidden virtual pointer) pointing to its class's vtable. When a virtual function is called through a base pointer/reference, the vptr is used to lookup the correct override at runtime. Virtual functions have performance overhead (indirect call, vtable lookup).

interviewQuestions.question: What is RAII and why is it important?
interviewQuestions.answer: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) ties resource lifetime to object lifetime: acquire resource in constructor, release in destructor. This ensures resources (memory, files, mutexes, sockets) are automatically freed when the object goes out of scope, even via exceptions. std::unique_ptr, std::lock_guard, std::ifstream are RAII examples. RAII is fundamental to exception-safe C++ code for leak-free and exception-safe resource management.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the Rule of Five in modern C++?
interviewQuestions.answer: Rule of Five states: if a class needs a custom destructor, copy constructor, copy assignment, move constructor, or move assignment, it usually needs all five. This follows from RAII — custom resource management requires explicit control of copying and moving. Default implementations do shallow copying which causes double-free or resource leaks for owning pointers. = default or = delete explicitly controls these special member functions.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain move semantics and rvalue references.
interviewQuestions.answer: Move semantics (C++11) allow efficiently transferring resources from temporary objects via rvalue references (T&&). std::move converts an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move instead of copy. Move operations steal resources (e.g., swapping pointer, invalidating source) instead of deep copying. This dramatically improves performance for expensive-to-copy objects (vectors, strings) by eliminating unnecessary copies in return values and temporary objects.

interviewQuestions.question: What are smart pointers and when to use each?
interviewQuestions.answer: Smart pointers (C++11) automate memory management: unique_ptr (exclusive ownership, non-copyable, lightweight), shared_ptr (shared ownership via reference counting, heavier), weak_ptr (non-owning observer that doesn't affect ref count, breaks circular references). Use unique_ptr as default for exclusive ownership. Use shared_ptr for shared ownership. Use weak_ptr to observe shared_ptr without extending lifetime. Never use raw owning pointers.

interviewQuestions.question: What is SFINAE and when is it used?
interviewQuestions.answer: SFINAE (Substitution Failure Is Not An Error) is a template metaprogramming principle where substituting template parameters may produce invalid types/code but the compiler doesn't error — it just removes that overload/specialization from consideration. Used with std::enable_if, decltype, and type traits to enable/disable function templates based on type properties. C++20 Concepts provide a more readable alternative to SFINAE.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism.
interviewQuestions.answer: Compile-time polymorphism: templates and function overloading — resolved at compile time, no runtime overhead, type-specific code generation. Runtime polymorphism: virtual functions and inheritance — resolved at runtime via vtable, dynamic dispatch overhead, binary interface stability. Templates are type-flexible and faster but increase code size. Virtual functions enable polymorphic behavior across compilation units.

interviewQuestions.question: What is undefined behavior in C++? Give examples.
interviewQuestions.answer: UB means the standard imposes no requirements on the program's behavior — anything can happen (crash, silent corruption, demons flying out of nose). Examples: null pointer dereference, buffer overflow, use-after-free, signed integer overflow, modifying const object, multiple definitions violating ODR, accessing through dangling reference. Tools: -fsanitize=undefined, Valgrind, static analysis. Undefined != unspecified/implementation-defined.

interviewQuestions.question: How do std::move and std::forward work?
interviewQuestions.answer: std::move unconditionally casts an lvalue to an rvalue reference, enabling move semantics. It's just a static_cast<T&&> with appropriate reference collapsing. std::forward conditionally casts to rvalue only if the original argument was an rvalue (perfect forwarding). Used in template functions to preserve argument value category. Both are compile-time casts with no runtime overhead.

interviewQuestions.question: What are C++20 Concepts and how do they improve templates?
interviewQuestions.answer: Concepts specify constraints on template parameters, providing: better error messages (compiler says 'T doesn't satisfy Sortable' instead of 200-line error), overload resolution based on type capabilities, and self-documenting code. Standard concepts: Sortable, Regular, Invocable, etc. Custom concepts use template<...> concept Name = requires(...) { ... }. Concepts replace most SFINAE use cases.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain coroutines in C++20.
interviewQuestions.answer: Coroutines (C++20) are functions that can suspend and resume execution using co_await, co_yield, and co_return. Implemented via promise_type, awaitable objects, and the coroutine handle. Types: generator (produce sequence), task (async computation), and async stream. Coroutines enable efficient async code without callback hell, making them ideal for I/O-bound, event-driven, and generator use cases.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between C++03 and C++11/14/17/20/23?
interviewQuestions.answer: C++11: auto, move semantics, lambdas, smart pointers, threading library, unordered containers. C++14: generic lambdas, constexpr relaxations, binary literals. C++17: structured bindings, if constexpr, filesystem, string_view, variants. C++20: concepts, coroutines, ranges, modules, spans, calendars, format. C++23: std::expected, stacktrace, mdspan, deducing this. Each revision makes C++ safer, more expressive, and easier to use.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the One Definition Rule (ODR)?
interviewQuestions.answer: ODR states: each variable, function, class, enum, template must have exactly one definition in the entire program (across all translation units). Multiple declarations are allowed. Violations cause linker errors (multiple definition) or undefined behavior (if definitions differ). Inline functions (inline keyword or constexpr/consteval) can have multiple identical definitions across TUs. Templates are defined in headers per ODR's allowance.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you prevent memory leaks in C++?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use RAII containers: std::vector, std::string over manual allocation. Use smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) for all dynamic allocations. Use containers from STL instead of C-style arrays. Follow Rule of Five for classes managing resources. Use valgrind/sanitizers (ASan, LSan) during development. Never use raw new/delete in application code. Use make_unique/make_shared instead of explicit new.

bestPractices: Prefer standard library facilities (std::vector, std::string) over raw arrays and C-style strings
Use RAII for all resource management — never manually manage resources with raw new/delete in application code
Use smart pointers (unique_ptr as default, shared_ptr only for shared ownership)
Use const whenever possible — const variables, const member functions, const parameters
Mark overriding functions with override (C++11) to catch signature mismatches at compile time
Use pass-by-value for cheaply copyable types, const reference for larger types, move for temporaries
Prefer enum class (C++11) over plain enum for type safety and scoping
Use range-based for loops instead of index-based loops when iterating containers
Follow Core Guidelines — use GSL (Guidelines Support Library) where applicable
Prefer auto for type deduction when the type is obvious from context
Use noexcept for functions that don't throw (enables optimizations, strengthens contract)
Initialize all variables — prefer {} initialization over () or = to avoid most-vexing-parse
Write constexpr whenever computation can happen at compile time
Use std::array instead of C-style arrays for fixed-size sequences
Enable compiler warnings (-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Werror) and use sanitizers
Organize code with header files (.hpp) and implementation files (.cpp); use #pragma once
Keep interfaces narrow — expose only what's necessary, use private/protected for internals
Use move semantics to eliminate unnecessary copies, especially in hot paths
Profile before optimizing — use compiler optimizations (-O2/-O3) and benchmark systematically

docker.name: Docker
docker.description: A platform for developing, shipping, and running applications in lightweight, portable containers. Standardizes application packaging and deployment across environments.
docker.overview: Docker revolutionized software development by making containers mainstream — packaging applications with all their dependencies into standardized, lightweight, and portable units. Launched in 2013, Docker uses OS-level virtualization to deliver software in packages called containers. Unlike VMs, containers share the host OS kernel, making them much more efficient. Docker provides tooling for building images (Dockerfile), sharing them (Docker Hub/registries), composing multi-service applications (Docker Compose), and orchestrating clusters (Docker Swarm, Kubernetes). The Docker ecosystem has become essential for DevOps, microservices, CI/CD pipelines, and cloud-native development. Docker ensures consistency across development, testing, and production environments — eliminating the 'it works on my machine' problem. Major cloud providers support Docker containers natively.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a Docker image and a container?
interviewQuestions.answer: An image is a read-only template with instructions for creating containers — the blueprint. A container is a runnable instance of an image — the actual running process. Images are built with Dockerfile, stored in registries. Containers have writable layers (copy-on-write), can be started, stopped, moved, and deleted. Multiple containers can run from the same image.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain Docker multi-stage builds and their benefits.
interviewQuestions.answer: Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements in a Dockerfile. Each stage can use different base images. Only files from the final stage end up in the final image. Benefits: dramatically smaller images (no build tools, SDKs, or temp files in production), separation of build and runtime environments, no need for multiple Dockerfiles. Example: compile Go in golang:alpine stage, copy binary to scratch stage.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Docker networking work? Explain bridge, host, and overlay.
interviewQuestions.answer: Bridge (default): containers on same bridge can communicate via IP, isolated from outside. Host: container shares host network stack, no isolation but better performance. Overlay: connects containers across multiple Docker hosts (Swarm mode). Bridge is for single-host container communication. Host for performance-critical apps needing direct network access. Overlay for multi-host microservices.

interviewQuestions.question: What is Docker Compose and when should you use it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker Compose defines and runs multi-container applications using a YAML file. It creates networks, volumes, and starts all services with one command. Use for: local development environments (app + DB + cache), testing infrastructure, CI services, simple production deployments (single host). Don't use for: multi-host deployments, production at scale (use Swarm/Kubernetes).

interviewQuestions.question: How do you manage persistent data in Docker?
interviewQuestions.answer: Volumes: managed by Docker, stored in /var/lib/docker/volumes, backup/restore possible, recommended for persistent data. Bind mounts: mount host directory into container, good for development (hot-reload). tmpfs: in-memory storage, temporary data not persisted. Volumes are preferred for databases, bind mounts for development code, tmpfs for scratch data.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Docker layers and how does caching work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Each Dockerfile instruction creates a layer. Docker caches each layer and reuses it on subsequent builds if the instruction and context haven't changed. Layer ordering matters: put infrequently-changing instructions first (package installs), then frequently-changing code last. Use .dockerignore to exclude unnecessary files from context. Cache invalidation: if a layer changes, all subsequent layers are rebuilt.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you secure a Docker container?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use minimal base images (Alpine, distroless), run as non-root user (USER directive), use read-only root filesystem, drop all capabilities then add needed ones (--cap-drop=ALL --cap-add=NET_BIND_SERVICE), use Docker Content Trust for image signing, scan images for vulnerabilities (Trivy, Snyk), use secrets management (Docker secrets, build secrets), set resource limits (memory, CPU), use security profiles (seccomp, AppArmor), enable user namespace remapping.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between CMD and ENTRYPOINT.
interviewQuestions.answer: Both specify what command runs when container starts. ENTRYPOINT defines the executable (fixed). CMD provides default arguments to ENTRYPOINT or a default command if no ENTRYPOINT. docker run arguments override CMD but append to ENTRYPOINT. Pattern: ENTRYPOINT ['executable'] with CMD ['default_args']. This allows easy overriding: docker run image --custom-flag.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you handle Docker container logs?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker captures stdout/stderr from container processes. Logging drivers: json-file (default, written as JSON files), syslog, journald, gelf, fluentd, awslogs, splunk, etwlogs, gcplogs, logentries. Configure via --log-driver. For production: use centralized logging (ELK, Loki, Datadog). Never log to files inside containers — logs disappear with container. Use json-file with rotation (--log-opt max-size=10m --log-opt max-file=3).

interviewQuestions.question: How does Docker handle resource constraints?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker uses cgroups to limit resources per container: --memory (RAM limit), --memory-reservation (soft limit), --cpus (CPU limit, fractional), --cpuset-cpus (specific CPU cores), --pids-limit (max processes). Overcommitting: sum of limits can exceed host capacity. OOM killer: Docker kills container exceeding memory limit. Use docker stats to monitor. Set resource limits on all production containers.

interviewQuestions.question: What is Docker Swarm and how does it compare to Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: Docker Swarm is Docker's native clustering and orchestration tool. Simpler setup, integrates with Docker CLI, uses YAML similar to Compose. Kubernetes has broader ecosystem, more features, better for complex deployments, larger community. Swarm is good for simpler deployments and teams already using Docker. K8s is better for large-scale, multi-cloud, complex orchestration needs.

java.name: Java
java.description: A class-based, object-oriented programming language designed for platform independence via the JVM. Powers enterprise applications, Android development, big data infrastructure, and large-scale backend systems.
java.overview: Java is one of the most widely used programming languages in the world, known for its 'write once, run anywhere' capability through the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Created by James Gosling at Sun Microsystems in 1995, Java has evolved through major versions with significant additions like lambdas (Java 8), modules (Java 9), pattern matching (Java 16+), and virtual threads (Java 21). It dominates enterprise backend development through the Spring ecosystem, powers Android apps, and forms the backbone of big data tools like Hadoop and Apache Spark. With strong typing, automatic memory management via garbage collection, and a vast mature ecosystem, Java remains a top choice for building reliable, scalable, high-performance applications.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between JDK, JRE, and JVM?
interviewQuestions.answer: JDK (Java Development Kit) includes JRE + development tools for compiling and debugging Java programs. JRE (Java Runtime Environment) includes JVM + core libraries for running Java applications. JVM (Java Virtual Machine) executes Java bytecode, providing platform independence.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between == and equals() in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: == compares reference equality (memory addresses) for objects, while equals() compares value equality. For primitive types, == compares values. The default equals() in Object uses ==, but classes like String, Integer override it to compare content.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain method overloading vs method overriding.
interviewQuestions.answer: Overloading is compile-time polymorphism where multiple methods share the same name but differ in parameters (number, type, or order) within the same class. Overriding is runtime polymorphism where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method already defined in its parent class, with same signature.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between abstract class and interface?
interviewQuestions.answer: Abstract classes can have constructors, instance variables, and both abstract and concrete methods; support single inheritance. Interfaces (pre-Java 8) had only abstract methods and constants; support multiple inheritance. Java 8+ interfaces can have default and static methods. Java 9+ allows private methods in interfaces. Abstract classes represent an 'is-a' relationship; interfaces represent a 'can-do' capability.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the final keyword in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: final can be applied to: variables (makes them constants, cannot be reassigned), methods (prevents overriding in subclasses), and classes (prevents inheritance). Blank final variables must be initialized in the constructor.

interviewQuestions.question: How does garbage collection work in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Java GC automatically reclaims memory from objects that are no longer reachable. The JVM periodically runs the GC, which identifies live objects (via root references) and removes unreachable ones. Common algorithms: Mark-Sweep, Mark-Compact, Copying. Modern JVMs use generational GC (Young, Old, Metaspace generations) with collectors like G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap?
interviewQuestions.answer: HashMap is not thread-safe; allows null keys/values; throws ConcurrentModificationException under concurrent modification. ConcurrentHashMap is thread-safe; does not allow null keys/values; uses lock striping (Java 7) or synchronized + CAS (Java 8+) for high concurrency; provides better performance under concurrent access.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Java memory model (JMM).
interviewQuestions.answer: JMM defines how threads interact through memory and specifies rules for visibility, ordering, and atomicity. It ensures that changes made by one thread become visible to others. Key concepts: happens-before relationship, volatile variables, synchronized blocks, and atomic operations. Without proper synchronization, threads may see stale values due to CPU caches and instruction reordering.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between synchronized and volatile in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: volatile ensures visibility of changes across threads (reads/writes happen directly to main memory) but does NOT provide atomicity or mutual exclusion. synchronized provides both visibility and atomicity by acquiring a monitor lock; it ensures mutual exclusion and establishes happens-before relationships. volatile is cheaper but limited; synchronized is more powerful but has higher overhead.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Spring Dependency Injection work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Spring DI inverts the control of object creation and wiring. The IoC Container (ApplicationContext) manages bean lifecycles. Dependencies are injected via constructor, setter, or field injection using annotations (@Autowired, @Inject). Spring resolves dependencies by type, then by name/qualifier. This promotes loose coupling, testability, and maintainability.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller?
interviewQuestions.answer: All are Spring stereotype annotations for marking beans. @Component is the generic stereotype. @Service marks service layer beans (business logic). @Repository marks DAO/repository beans; adds persistence exception translation. @Controller marks web layer beans in Spring MVC; supports request mapping. They are functionally identical but communicate intent and enable specific behavior (like @Repository's exception translation).

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Stream API and its intermediate/terminal operations.
interviewQuestions.answer: Stream API in Java 8 enables functional-style operations on collections. Intermediate operations (map, filter, sorted, distinct, limit, skip, flatMap) return a new stream and are lazy. Terminal operations (collect, forEach, reduce, count, anyMatch, allMatch, findFirst, findAny) produce a result or side-effect and close the stream. Streams cannot be reused after a terminal operation.

interviewQuestions.question: What are the different types of garbage collectors in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Major GC types: Serial GC (single-threaded, for small apps), Parallel/Throughput GC (multi-threaded, for batch jobs), CMS (low-latency, deprecated in Java 14), G1 (default since Java 9, balanced throughput/latency for large heaps), ZGC (ultra-low latency, scalable, Java 15+), Shenandoah (low-pause, concurrent compaction, Java 12+). ZGC and Shenandoah aim for sub-millisecond pause times.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Spring Boot auto-configuration uses @EnableAutoConfiguration combined with spring.factories/META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports files that list configuration classes. @Conditional annotations (like @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, @ConditionalOnProperty) control whether configurations activate based on classpath contents, property settings, bean presence, etc.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between @Transactional in Spring and manual transaction management?
interviewQuestions.answer: @Transactional is declarative transaction management; Spring wraps the method with AOP, handling begin, commit, and rollback automatically based on RuntimeException. Manual management uses PlatformTransactionManager API (beginTransaction, commit, rollback). Declarative is cleaner but offers less granular control. @Transactional supports propagation, isolation, timeout, readOnly, and rollbackFor attributes.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain Project Loom and Virtual Threads.
interviewQuestions.answer: Project Loom introduces virtual threads (Java 21+) — lightweight threads managed by the JVM rather than the OS. Virtual threads are cheap enough to create millions per application, enabling simpler thread-per-request models without the overhead of OS threads. They block on I/O by yielding their carrier thread, dramatically improving resource utilization under high concurrency.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the Java Module System (JPMS) work?
interviewQuestions.answer: JPMS (Java 9+) introduces modules — named groups of related packages with explicit dependencies and exported packages. module-info.java declares module name, requires (dependencies), exports (accessible packages), opens (for reflection), provides (service implementations), and uses (service dependencies). JPMS enforces encapsulation at the JVM level, eliminates classpath issues, and enables reliable configuration.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the Fork/Join framework in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Fork/Join (Java 7+) implements the work-stealing algorithm for parallel processing. It splits (forks) a task into subtasks recursively, processes them in parallel via a pool of worker threads, then joins results. The ForkJoinPool uses a work-stealing approach where idle threads steal tasks from busy threads' queues. It's ideal for divide-and-conquer algorithms like parallel sorting, search, and matrix operations.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Java Records and when should you use them?
interviewQuestions.answer: Records (Java 16) are transparent, immutable data carrier classes. They automatically generate constructor, equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and accessor methods. Use records for DTOs, value objects, and any class whose main purpose is carrying immutable data. Records cannot extend other classes, cannot have instance fields besides the components, and are implicitly final.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain sealed classes in Java.
interviewQuestions.answer: Sealed classes (Java 17) restrict which classes or interfaces may extend/implement them. Declared with the sealed keyword followed by permits listing permitted subclasses. Sealed classes enable exhaustive pattern matching by limiting the inheritance hierarchy. Permitted subclasses must be final, sealed, or non-sealed. This provides more control over the type hierarchy than the traditional all-or-nothing approach.

interviewQuestions.question: What is pattern matching for instanceof?
interviewQuestions.answer: Pattern matching for instanceof (Java 16+) combines type checking and variable binding into a single expression. Instead of: if (obj instanceof String) { String s = (String) obj; }, you write: if (obj instanceof String s) { /* use s directly */ }. The pattern variable s is only in scope where the instanceof check succeeds, eliminating boilerplate and potential casting errors.

interviewQuestions.question: How does reactive programming differ from imperative programming in Java?
interviewQuestions.answer: Reactive programming is declarative and data-flow driven vs imperative's step-by-step execution. Reactive uses: Reactive Streams specification (Publisher, Subscriber, Subscription, Processor), backpressure for flow control, Project Reactor (Mono for 0-1 elements, Flux for 0-N elements), operators for transformation (map, flatMap, filter), and non-blocking I/O. WebFlux provides reactive web capabilities on top of Reactor compared to traditional blocking Servlet API.

interviewQuestions.question: What are virtual threads and how do they differ from platform threads?
interviewQuestions.answer: Virtual threads (Java 21, Project Loom) are lightweight JVM-managed threads that multiplex onto platform (OS) threads. Unlike platform threads which consume ~1MB of stack and are limited to thousands, virtual threads cost ~few KB and can be created in millions. They follow the thread-per-request model without the overhead. When a virtual thread blocks on I/O, it yields its carrier thread to run other virtual threads, dramatically improving throughput for I/O-bound applications.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions with examples.
interviewQuestions.answer: Checked exceptions (subclasses of Exception but not RuntimeException) must be declared in throws clause or caught. Examples: IOException, SQLException, ClassNotFoundException. They represent recoverable conditions the caller should handle. Unchecked exceptions (subclasses of RuntimeException and Error) don't require handling. Examples: NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException. They represent programming errors or unrecoverable conditions. Modern Java frameworks prefer unchecked exceptions to avoid cluttering code.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe iterators?
interviewQuestions.answer: Fail-fast iterators (e.g., ArrayList.iterator()) throw ConcurrentModificationException if the collection is structurally modified during iteration (except via iterator's own remove method). They work by tracking a modCount field. Fail-safe or weakly consistent iterators (e.g., ConcurrentHashMap.keySet().iterator()) allow concurrent modification by iterating over a snapshot or a copy of the data, thus never throwing ConcurrentModificationException but may not reflect latest modifications.

interviewQuestions.question: What are the new switch expressions in Java 14+?
interviewQuestions.answer: Switch expressions (Java 14 standard) extend switch statements with: arrow syntax (->) instead of colon-break, no fall-through with arrows, ability to return values (use with assignment), multiple case labels (case 1, 2, 3 ->), and exhaustive checking (must cover all cases, use default if not exhaustive). Switch expressions are more concise and less error-prone than traditional switch statements.

interviewQuestions.question: Describe the different states in a thread lifecycle.
interviewQuestions.answer: Thread states: NEW (created but not started), RUNNABLE (executing or ready to execute), BLOCKED (waiting for a monitor lock), WAITING (waiting indefinitely for another thread — Object.wait(), Thread.join() without timeout), TIMED_WAITING (waiting with timeout — Thread.sleep(), Object.wait(timeout)), TERMINATED (execution finished). A thread can be in only one state at a time, and transitions are controlled by the JVM.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the concept of method references in Java 8.
interviewQuestions.answer: Method references are shorthand for lambda expressions calling an existing method. Four types: static method reference (Class::staticMethod), instance method of a particular object (instance::method), instance method of any object of a particular type (Class::instanceMethod), and constructor reference (Class::new). Example: list.forEach(System.out::println) is equivalent to list.forEach(s -> System.out.println(s)). Method references are more concise and readable.

bestPractices: Always use meaningful, descriptive names for classes, methods, and variables following Java naming conventions (PascalCase for classes, camelCase for methods/variables, UPPER_SNAKE_CASE for constants)
Follow the Single Responsibility Principle — each class should have one reason to change
Prefer composition over inheritance to achieve code reuse and flexibility
Use immutable objects where possible (final class, private fields, no setters, defensive copying in constructors)
Always override equals() and hashCode() together when using objects in collections
Avoid null returns — use Optional, empty collections, or the Null Object pattern
Close resources properly — use try-with-resources for AutoCloseable resources (streams, connections, files)
Program to interfaces, not implementations, for loose coupling
Keep methods short and focused — a method should do one thing and do it well
Minimize mutability — prefer final fields and immutable data structures
Use StringBuilder/StringBuffer for string concatenation in loops
Catch specific exceptions, never catch generic Exception or Throwable
Use Spring Boot's constructor injection over field injection for better testability
Write unit tests first (TDD) and aim for high coverage on business logic
Cache frequently accessed data with proper TTL and invalidation strategies
Profile before optimizing — always measure performance before making changes
Use SLF4J with parameterized logging instead of string concatenation
Keep configuration external — use application.yml/properties, environment variables, or configuration servers
Version your APIs with semantic versioning and never break backward compatibility in the same major version
Use Java modules (JPMS) for large applications to enforce strong encapsulation

javascript.name: JavaScript
javascript.description: The language of the web — a high-level, dynamic, interpreted language powering interactive websites, server-side applications, mobile apps, and desktop software.
javascript.overview: JavaScript is the most widely-used programming language in the world, running on virtually every web browser and increasingly on servers (Node.js), desktops (Electron), and mobile devices (React Native). Created by Brendan Eich in 1995, JavaScript has evolved from a simple scripting language into a mature, multi-paradigm language with first-class functions, prototypal inheritance, async/await, modules, and a vibrant ecosystem. The ECMAScript specification (ES6/ES2015 through ES2024) brings yearly improvements. JavaScript's single-threaded event loop model, closures, and functional capabilities make it uniquely suited for interactive web applications. With npm's massive package ecosystem (2M+ packages), JavaScript developers can leverage solutions for virtually any problem.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the difference between var, let, and const.
interviewQuestions.answer: var: function-scoped, hoisted (initialized as undefined), can be redeclared and reassigned. let: block-scoped, hoisted but not initialized (Temporal Dead Zone), can be reassigned but not redeclared in same scope. const: block-scoped, hoisted but not initialized, must be initialized at declaration, cannot be reassigned (but object properties can be mutated). Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed, never var in modern JS.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the event loop and how does it work?
interviewQuestions.answer: The event loop is JavaScript's concurrency model. It continuously checks if the call stack is empty. When empty, it processes tasks from the task queue (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O) and microtask queue (Promise.then, catch, finally, queueMicrotask, MutationObserver). Microtasks have priority over tasks. The event loop enables non-blocking async operations despite JavaScript being single-threaded.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain closures with a practical example.
interviewQuestions.answer: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its outer (enclosing) scope even after the outer function has returned. Example: function makeCounter() { let count = 0; return function() { return ++count; }; } const counter = makeCounter(); counter() // 1, counter() // 2. Uses: data privacy, module pattern, event handlers, memoization, factory functions.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: == (abstract equality) compares after type coercion, applying complex conversion rules. === (strict equality) compares without type coercion — returns false if types differ. Always use === to avoid unexpected coercion bugs. Exception: x == null safely checks both null and undefined. Key coercion: '' == 0 is true, false == 'false' is false, null == undefined is true.

interviewQuestions.question: What is prototypal inheritance?
interviewQuestions.answer: JavaScript uses prototypal inheritance where objects inherit directly from other objects via the prototype chain. Each object has an internal [[Prototype]] (accessible via __proto__ or Object.getPrototypeOf). When accessing a property, JS traverses the chain until found. Object.create() creates objects with a specified prototype. ES6 classes are syntactic sugar over prototypal inheritance with constructor functions.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the concept of Hoisting.
interviewQuestions.answer: Hoisting is JS's behavior of moving declarations to the top of their scope during compilation. var declarations are hoisted and initialized to undefined. let/const declarations are hoisted but not initialized (Temporal Dead Zone — ReferenceError if accessed before declaration). Function declarations are fully hoisted (definition available). Function expressions (var/let/const) follow their variable hoisting rules.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the this keyword work in JavaScript?
interviewQuestions.answer: this depends on execution context: global context — window (browser) / global (Node); function — window (strict: undefined); method — the calling object; arrow function — lexical this (from enclosing scope); constructor (new) — the new instance; event handler — the element; explicit: call, apply, bind set this manually; class methods — the class instance. Understanding this is critical for avoiding common bugs.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Promises and how do they differ from callbacks?
interviewQuestions.answer: Promises represent eventual completion (or failure) of an async operation. States: pending, fulfilled, rejected. Benefits over callbacks: chaining instead of nesting (avoid callback hell), unified error handling (.catch()), composition (Promise.all, Promise.race), built-in cancellation with AbortController. A Promise can only resolve/reject once and maintains its state, unlike callbacks which can be called multiple times.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between null and undefined?
interviewQuestions.answer: undefined means a variable has been declared but not assigned a value (default). null is an intentional absence of any object value — must be explicitly assigned. typeof undefined is 'undefined', typeof null is 'object' (historical bug). null == undefined is true (== coercion), null === undefined is false. Function parameters default to undefined. JSON doesn't support undefined but supports null.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain debouncing and throttling.
interviewQuestions.answer: Debouncing delays execution until after a specified quiet period — useful for search input (wait until user stops typing). Throttling limits execution to at most once per specified interval — useful for scroll/resize handlers. Both improve performance for frequent events. Debounce: clearTimeout on each call, setTimeout on last. Throttle: use timestamp or flag to track last execution.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between LocalStorage, SessionStorage, and Cookies?
interviewQuestions.answer: LocalStorage: persists until explicitly cleared, ~5MB, synchronous, not sent to server. SessionStorage: persists only for the current tab/window session, cleared on close, ~5MB. Cookies: sent with every HTTP request, max 4KB, can set expiry, supports HttpOnly/Secure flags. For client-only data use localStorage/sessionStorage. For auth tokens use httpOnly cookies. IndexedDB is used for larger structured data.

interviewQuestions.question: How does async/await work under the hood?
interviewQuestions.answer: Async/await is syntactic sugar over Promises + generators. An async function returns a Promise. await pauses execution of the async function, yielding control back to the event loop, and resumes when the awaited Promise resolves (or throws on rejection). The compiler transforms async functions into state machines using generators (or directly into Promises). Each await is a suspension point.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Module pattern and its benefits.
interviewQuestions.answer: The Module pattern uses closures to create private scopes and expose a public API. Benefits: encapsulation (private variables/methods), namespace isolation (avoids global pollution), dependency management, maintainability. Modern JS uses ES modules (import/export) natively. IIFE-based modules created the pattern before native module support. Revealing Module pattern assigns public references to private functions.

interviewQuestions.question: What are Web Workers and when would you use them?
interviewQuestions.answer: Web Workers run JavaScript in background threads separate from the main execution thread. They communicate via postMessage and onmessage. Use cases: CPU-intensive calculations, image/video processing, large data parsing, compression, and tasks that shouldn't block UI. Limitations: no DOM access, no window/document, limited APIs. SharedWorkers can be shared across multiple tabs. Service Workers are a special type for network proxying.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you optimize JavaScript performance?
interviewQuestions.answer: Techniques: minimize DOM access (batch reads/writes, virtual DOM), use event delegation instead of many handlers, debounce/throttle frequent events, use requestAnimationFrame for visual updates, avoid layout thrashing, code-split with dynamic imports, lazy load images/components, use Web Workers for heavy computation, avoid memory leaks (clean up listeners/timeouts), use performance.now() and Chrome DevTools Performance tab.

bestPractices: Use const by default, let when reassignment is needed — never use var in modern code
Use strict equality (===) and strict inequality (!==) instead of == and !=
Prefer arrow functions for callbacks and when preserving lexical this, use named functions for standalone definitions
Use template literals instead of string concatenation
Use object and array destructuring for cleaner code
Use default parameters instead of manual undefined checks
Use optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??) for safer property access
Keep functions small and focused on single responsibility
Use early returns to reduce nesting
Prefer map/filter/reduce over for loops for array transformations
Use async/await over raw Promise chains for readability
Handle promise rejections with .catch() or try/catch in async functions
Avoid mutation — create new arrays/objects with spread or methods
Use named exports over default exports for better refactoring
Organize code by feature (not by type) in larger codebases
Write self-documenting code — use meaningful variable and function names
Use JSDoc comments for public API documentation
Use ESLint and Prettier to enforce consistent code style
Write tests (Jest, Testing Library) for critical business logic
Use TypeScript for any project with more than a few files

kubernetes.name: Kubernetes
kubernetes.description: An open-source container orchestration platform for automating deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications. The industry standard for production container orchestration.
kubernetes.overview: Kubernetes (K8s) is the leading open-source platform for managing containerized workloads and services. Originating from Google's internal Borg system (15+ years of production experience), Kubernetes was open-sourced in 2014 and has become the standard API for cloud-native computing. It automates container deployment, scaling, load balancing, service discovery, rolling updates, self-healing, and resource management across clusters. Kubernetes abstracts underlying infrastructure, enabling consistent deployments across on-premise, cloud, and hybrid environments. Its extensible architecture allows integration with service meshes (Istio), monitoring (Prometheus), CI/CD (ArgoCD), and serverless (Knative). While powerful, Kubernetes has a steep learning curve due to its complex architecture, extensive configuration options, and new paradigms (declarative vs imperative, desired state vs current state). Mastery of Kubernetes is essential for modern platform engineering and cloud-native development.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a Pod and why is it the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: A Pod is the smallest deployable unit that represents a single instance of a running process in Kubernetes. It wraps one or more containers with shared storage/network (same IP, port space), and a specification for how to run them (resources, restart policy, probes). Pods enable: co-located helper containers (sidecars), shared volumes between containers, and unified lifecycle management. Containers in a pod are always scheduled on the same node.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a Deployment and a StatefulSet?
interviewQuestions.answer: Deployment: for stateless applications — pods are interchangeable, can be created/destroyed in any order, without persistent identity. StatefulSet: for stateful applications — each pod gets a unique, stable identity (ordinal index), ordered creation/scaling/deletion, persistent storage per pod via volumeClaimTemplates. Use Deployment for stateless apps (web servers, APIs). Use StatefulSet for databases, message queues, and any app needing stable network identity.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Kubernetes service discovery work?
interviewQuestions.answer: Kubernetes provides DNS-based service discovery via CoreDNS. Each Service gets a DNS name: <service>.<namespace>.svc.cluster.local. Pods resolve service names to ClusterIPs. For headless services (clusterIP: None), DNS returns pod IPs directly. kube-proxy implements virtual IPs (iptables/IPVS) and load balancing. Service environment variables (SERVICE_HOST, SERVICE_PORT) are injected but DNS is preferred.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the Kubernetes controller pattern.
interviewQuestions.answer: Controllers are control loops that watch the state of resources via the API server and make changes to drive the current state toward the desired state (reconciliation loop). Examples: ReplicaSet controller ensures correct number of pod replicas, Deployment controller manages ReplicaSet rollouts, EndpointSlice controller updates endpoints when pods change. Custom controllers can be built for custom resources (operator pattern).

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a Service and an Ingress?
interviewQuestions.answer: Service: internal load balancer providing stable network endpoint for pods, operates at L4 (TCP/UDP), types: ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer. Ingress: external HTTP/HTTPS routing to services, operates at L7, provides host/path-based routing, TLS termination, and name-based virtual hosting. Ingress requires an Ingress Controller (NGINX, Traefik, HAProxy) to be installed in the cluster.

interviewQuestions.question: How does Kubernetes handle persistent storage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Storage is managed through PV (PersistentVolume) and PVC (PersistentVolumeClaim). PV is cluster storage resource provisioned by admin or dynamically via StorageClass. PVC is a request for storage by a user — binds to matching PV. Pods use PVC as volumes. StorageClass enables dynamic provisioning (cloud volumes: EBS, EFS, GCEPersistentDisk, AzureDisk). accessModes: ReadWriteOnce, ReadOnlyMany, ReadWriteMany.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the role of etcd in Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: etcd is a consistent, distributed key-value store that stores all cluster state and configuration — the single source of truth. It stores: pod specs, service definitions, ConfigMaps, Secrets, RBAC policies, cluster state. The API server is the only component that talks to etcd. Backup etcd regularly — losing etcd means losing the cluster. etcd uses RAFT consensus for consistency across nodes.

interviewQuestions.question: How does the scheduler assign pods to nodes?
interviewQuestions.answer: The scheduler watches for unscheduled pods (pod.spec.nodeName is empty) and assigns them to optimal nodes through a two-phase process: Filtering (predicates): remove nodes that don't meet pod requirements (resource requests, nodeSelector, affinity/anti-affinity, taints/tolerations). Scoring (priorities): rank remaining nodes by various criteria (resource availability, spreading, affinity, etc.). The highest-scoring node wins.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a Helm chart and why use it?
interviewQuestions.answer: A Helm chart is a package of pre-configured Kubernetes resources. It provides: templating (reusable, parameterized YAML), dependency management (subcharts), versioning (install/upgrade/rollback), release management (deploy same chart multiple times). Charts are structured: Chart.yaml (metadata), values.yaml (default config), templates/ (Kubernetes YAML with Go templates). Use Helm to package, share, and deploy complex applications.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the GitOps workflow with ArgoCD.
interviewQuestions.answer: GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for declarative infrastructure and applications. ArgoCD: pull-based GitOps tool that continuously monitors a Git repository. When the Git repo changes, ArgoCD syncs the cluster to match. Benefits: audit trail (all changes via Git PR), easy rollback (git revert), single source of truth, automated drift correction. ArgoCD features: Application CRD, sync waves, hooks, SSO, multiple clusters.

interviewQuestions.question: What are taints and tolerations in Kubernetes?
interviewQuestions.answer: Taints are applied to nodes to repel pods that don't tolerate the taint. Tolerations are applied to pods to allow scheduling on tainted nodes. Taint effect: NoSchedule (don't schedule new pods), PreferNoSchedule (try to avoid), NoExecute (evict existing pods without toleration). Use cases: dedicated nodes (GPU-only), node isolation, node maintenance. taint: key=value:Effect, toleration: operator: Equal/Exists.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you upgrade a Kubernetes cluster?
interviewQuestions.answer: Upgrade order: first upgrade the control plane (one minor version at a time), then upgrade worker nodes. Steps: drain nodes (evict pods gracefully), upgrade kubeadm/kubelet/kubectl on each node, uncordon the node. Version skew policy: kubelet can be up to 2 minor versions behind the control plane. Always backup etcd before upgrades. Use managed K8s (EKS, AKS, GKE) for automated upgrades.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between RBAC roles and cluster roles?
interviewQuestions.answer: Role: grants permissions within a specific namespace. ClusterRole: grants permissions cluster-wide (non-namespaced resources, namespaced resources across all namespaces). RoleBinding: binds a Role to subjects (users, groups, service accounts) within a namespace. ClusterRoleBinding: binds a ClusterRole cluster-wide. Pattern: use ClusterRole for read-only access to all namespaces, Role for namespace-specific permissions.

bestPractices: Set resource requests and limits on all containers — never rely on default unbounded resources
Use namespaces for environment isolation (dev, staging, prod) with ResourceQuotas
Use liveness, readiness, and startup probes for every pod for self-healing
Use PodDisruptionBudgets for critical workloads to maintain availability during node maintenance
Follow the principle of least privilege with RBAC — cluster-admin is for cluster-wide operators only
Use multiple replicas (>=2) for production workloads for high availability
Use rolling updates with maxSurge and maxUnavailable for zero-downtime deployments
Use infrastructure as code — store all manifests in Git, use GitOps (ArgoCD/Flux)
Use Helm or Kustomize for managing application configurations across environments
Use network policies to restrict pod-to-pod communication (zero-trust network model)
Use Secret resources (not ConfigMaps) for sensitive data — enable encryption at rest
Use external secrets operators (External Secrets, Sealed Secrets) for production secret management
Use pod anti-affinity and topology spread constraints for high availability across nodes/zones
Never run containers as root — set securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true
Use readiness probe for rolling updates — ensure new pods accept traffic before old ones terminate
Use PodSecurityAdmission to enforce security standards (restricted profile for user workloads)
Monitor everything — cluster metrics, pod metrics, logs, events, with proper alerting
Use PDBs for stateful workloads to prevent data loss during voluntary disruptions
Use init containers for setup tasks that must complete before app containers start
Regularly backup etcd — losing etcd means losing the entire cluster state

ml.name: Machine Learning
ml.description: A subset of AI focused on algorithms that learn patterns from data and make predictions or decisions without explicit programming. Powers recommendation systems, fraud detection, and predictive analytics.
ml.overview: Machine Learning is the scientific study of algorithms and statistical models that computer systems use to progressively improve their performance on a specific task through experience (data). Arthur Samuel coined the term in 1959. ML is divided into supervised learning (learning from labeled examples), unsupervised learning (finding hidden patterns in unlabeled data), and reinforcement learning (learning through interaction). The field has advanced from linear models to complex deep neural networks, gradient boosting (XGBoost), and foundation models. ML powers modern recommendation systems, search engines, fraud detection, medical diagnosis, autonomous vehicles, and language models. The ML workflow involves data collection, cleaning, feature engineering, model selection, training, evaluation, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment. ML has democratized AI, enabling practitioners to build intelligent systems without being AI researchers.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the bias-variance tradeoff with examples.
interviewQuestions.answer: High bias (underfitting): model makes strong assumptions, e.g., linear regression on non-linear data — misses patterns. High variance (overfitting): model is too sensitive to training data, e.g., deep decision tree — captures noise. Increasing model complexity increases variance but decreases bias. The sweet spot minimizes total error (bias² + variance + irreducible error). Cross-validation helps find the optimal complexity.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between bagging and boosting?
interviewQuestions.answer: Bagging (Random Forest): parallel training of independent models on bootstrap samples, reduces variance. Boosting (XGBoost): sequential training where each model corrects predecessor's errors, reduces bias. Bagging is good for high-variance models. Boosting risks overfitting but often achieves higher accuracy. Both reduce different components of error.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain gradient descent and its variants.
interviewQuestions.answer: Batch GD: computes gradient on entire dataset — accurate but slow for large data. Stochastic GD (SGD): computes gradient on one sample — fast but noisy updates. Mini-batch GD: computes gradient on small batch — balance between speed and stability. Challenges: learning rate selection, saddle points, local minima. Variants: Momentum (accelerates in consistent direction), RMSprop (adapts LR per parameter), Adam (Momentum + RMSprop).

interviewQuestions.question: What is cross-validation and why use it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Cross-validation (CV) evaluates model generalization by training on subsets and validating on held-out data. k-fold CV splits data into k folds, training on k-1 and validating on 1, repeating k times. Benefits: more reliable performance estimate than single split, reduces variance of evaluation, all data used for training and validation. Stratified CV preserves class proportions. Time series CV respects temporal order.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you handle imbalanced datasets?
interviewQuestions.answer: Data-level: oversampling minority (SMOTE, ADASYN, random oversampling), undersampling majority (RandomUnderSampler, TomekLinks, Edited Nearest Neighbors). Algorithm-level: class weights, cost-sensitive learning, anomaly detection framing. Evaluation: use precision/recall/F1, ROC-AUC, PR-AUC instead of accuracy. Threshold: adjust decision threshold (not default 0.5). Ensemble: balanced bagging, EasyEnsemble, BalanceCascade.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between L1 and L2 regularization?
interviewQuestions.answer: L1 (Lasso): adds sum of absolute weights to loss — produces sparse solutions (many weights become zero), useful for feature selection. L2 (Ridge): adds sum of squared weights to loss — shrinks weights but rarely to zero, handles multicollinearity well. ElasticNet: combination of both. L1 is more robust to outliers. L2 has analytical solution. Both reduce overfitting by penalizing large weights.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the working of Random Forest.
interviewQuestions.answer: Random Forest builds multiple decision trees on bootstrap samples of the data. At each split, only a random subset of features is considered (feature bagging). Each tree votes, and majority decides (classification) or average gives output (regression). Benefits: handles high-dimensional data, estimates feature importance, OOB error for validation, resistant to overfitting, handles missing values. n_estimators and max_features are key hyperparameters.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the kernel trick in SVMs?
interviewQuestions.answer: The kernel trick allows SVMs to operate in a high-dimensional feature space without explicitly computing coordinates — instead computing dot products via kernel function. Common kernels: linear (no transformation), polynomial (degree d), RBF/Gaussian (infinite-dimensional mapping), sigmoid. RBF is default — maps to infinite dimensions, handles non-linear boundaries. Kernel choice depends on data structure and problem.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you detect and prevent data leakage?
interviewQuestions.answer: Data leakage occurs when information from outside the training set (e.g., future data, target information) influences the model. Sources: using target to create features, scaling before splitting, temporal leakage (future info leaked), train/test contamination. Prevention: split before any preprocessing, use pipelines (scikit-learn Pipeline), no global statistics, careful feature engineering, temporal ordering for time series, separate feature/target computation.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain PCA: when and why to use it.
interviewQuestions.answer: PCA (Principal Component Analysis) finds orthogonal directions (principal components) of maximum variance and projects data onto them. Uses: dimensionality reduction (reduce features while retaining variance), noise reduction (discard low-variance components), visualization (2D/3D), decorrelation of features. PCA is sensitive to feature scaling (always standardize first). Choose number of components by explained variance ratio.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between parametric and non-parametric models?
interviewQuestions.answer: Parametric models (linear regression, logistic regression, Naive Bayes) assume fixed functional form with finite parameters — faster training, less data required, but limited flexibility. Non-parametric models (k-NN, decision trees, SVMs, Random Forest) don't assume specific distribution — complexity grows with data, more flexible, but require more data and computational resources.

bestPractices: Always split data before any preprocessing to prevent data leakage — use scikit-learn Pipeline
Start with simple models (linear regression, logistic regression, decision trees) — they're good baselines
Use domain knowledge for feature engineering — the best features often come from understanding the problem
Standardize features for gradient-based and distance-based models
Use cross-validation (preferably stratified k-fold) for reliable performance estimates
Monitor for data leakage: no future data, no target information in features, split before scaling
Log all experiments: hyperparameters, metrics, code version, data version
Use version control for both code (Git) and data (DVC)
Prefer simpler models when performance is comparable — Occam's Razor
Use ensembles (stacking, blending) for final models but understand they add complexity
Fix all data issues before model building — garbage in, garbage out
Set reproducible seeds for all random operations (numpy, sklearn, pytorch)
Use Optuna or hyperopt for hyperparameter tuning — better than grid/random search
Check model assumptions for statistical models (linearity, normality, homoscedasticity)
Test on out-of-distribution data to check generalization
Document model decisions, assumptions, and limitations
Implement model monitoring — data drift and concept drift can degrade performance silently
Use appropriate metrics: PR-AUC for imbalanced, RMSE for regression, etc.
Start with a simple deployable model and iterate — perfect is the enemy of good in production ML

nodejs.name: Node.js
nodejs.description: A JavaScript runtime built on Chrome's V8 engine for building fast, scalable server-side applications. Powers backend services, APIs, real-time apps, CLIs, and microservices.
nodejs.overview: Node.js, created by Ryan Dahl in 2009, revolutionized JavaScript by bringing it to the server side. Built on Chrome's V8 JavaScript engine, Node uses an event-driven, non-blocking I/O model that makes it lightweight and efficient for data-intensive real-time applications. Its package ecosystem (npm) is the largest in the world with over 2 million packages. Node.js excels at building RESTful APIs, real-time services (WebSockets), CLI tools, and microservices. The introduction of ES modules, worker threads, and performance improvements in recent versions has solidified Node.js as a premier backend choice. Major companies like Netflix, LinkedIn, Uber, and PayPal run Node.js in production. Node.js is also the foundation for frontend tooling (Vite, Webpack, Babel) and desktop apps (Electron).
